q3: The comment-in-code problem kept resurfacing — the model understood the feedback when I gave it explicitly, but it didn't anticipate that a comment restating what the prose already covered is noise, not signal. It took direct correction to get there rather than the model catching it proactively.
q5: I looked at it, and it maps pretty well to what I care about — the rejected-alternative framing being priority one is exactly right, and surfacing warnings before code is something I feel strongly about. That said, the rubric phrases things in a way that's more analytical than how I actually think about it when I'm reading a draft, so I'm not sure the model was using it the same way I would.
q6: It made me notice that the model is reasonably good at structural fixes — moving warnings, adding rejected alternatives — but less reliable on the subtler prose-code relationship stuff. The rubric has a criterion for prose-code non-redundancy, and that's exactly where the model kept slipping, which suggests the rubric identified a real weak point even if the model didn't fully close the gap on it.

q3: The closing sentence kept landing slightly off — it wanted to make a broader claim than the evidence supported, and I had to push a few times to get it to commit to the honest, bounded version. The model understood the structure fine but had a tendency to reach for formal sign-off language where plain accuracy was better.
q5: I looked at it, and it's a reasonably accurate map of what I care about — the rejected-alternative framing at the top is right, and 'tradeoffs must name the specific cost' is exactly the distinction that matters. What it doesn't quite capture is the placement logic for warnings, which I think of as a timing problem, not just a prominence problem. Close enough to be useful, not complete.
q6: Seeing the rubric made me notice that the model was consistently good at structure and consistently slow on precision in closing statements — it would nail the architecture sections and then soften the landing. I probably would have chalked that up to individual sentences being off rather than recognizing it as a pattern. The rubric made it easier to see that the issue was about claim scope, not just word choice.

q3: The diagnostic paragraph tone was the persistent sticking point — the model kept getting the content right but the register wrong, writing like someone describing a situation from outside rather than someone who'd actually been in it. Even with explicit feedback it took multiple iterations to land.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about pretty well — the rejected-alternative framing, warnings before code, minimal examples. The one place it fell short is that 'Respects the Reader's Intelligence' criterion, which describes the goal but doesn't quite capture the specific failure mode I kept pushing back on: the difference between acknowledging difficulty in words versus actually sounding like you've been there.
q6: The rubric made it clearer that the model is good at structural compliance — it hit the rejected-alternative framing and warning placement consistently — but tone is where it struggles. It can describe the right emotional register without actually writing in it, and without the rubric I might have just attributed that to vague 'voice' issues rather than seeing it as a consistent pattern across iterations.
